changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "The Notebook's Beginning"

TextENGNew: A certain day in June, 1982.
changeReason: New translation includes "a certain day" (某日), specifying this refers to a particular unidentified date, while old just says "June, 1982."

TextENGNew: Satoshi Houjou disappeared several days after the Watanagashi festival.
Thinking rationally, there are three possible explanations for his disappearance.
The first is an accident of some kind.
It is not unheard of for someone struck by a car to end up in an irrigation ditch, their body not discovered for months.
However, the police conducted a thorough search of the areas Satoshi-kun frequented, and since nothing was found, this seems unlikely.
The second possibility is voluntary disappearance.
Satoshi-kun had been living under extreme psychological pressure.
changeReason: New translation specifies "extreme psychological pressure" matching JPN "精神的に非常に追い詰められた," while old uses the vaguer "dire circumstances" which omits the psychological aspect.

TextENGNew: In fact, he reportedly let slip to those around him that he wanted to vanish.
The police believed he had killed his aunt and fled.
Considering all these factors, that seemed the most plausible explanation.
However, after another suspect was identified as the actual killer, this theory was ruled out.
The final possibility is that he fell victim to the Hinamizawa serial mysterious deaths -- commonly known as the curse of Oyashiro-sama -- and was made to disappear.
changeReason: New translation uses "was made to disappear," implying someone caused the disappearance, matching the context of this theory about the Oyashiro-sama curse being human-engineered, while old simply says "disappeared."

TextENGNew: Unless the occult existence of Oyashiro-sama can be proven, these incidents are unquestionably the work of human hands.
changeReason: Old translates "事件" (incidents) as "curse," while new correctly renders it as "incidents," changing what is attributed to human agency.

TextENGNew: The most reasonable conclusion is that Satoshi-kun was eliminated by someone.
If so, the crucial question becomes: by whom?
To state my conclusion upfront:
changeReason: JPN "結論から言うと" means "to state the conclusion first," a rhetorical framing device. The old translation invents a different meaning ("The possibilities are quite limited").

TextENGNew: The culprit is almost certainly either the Sonozaki main family itself, or someone among the Three Families or their relatives who acted on the Sonozakis' wishes.
changeReason: New translation captures "その意向を汲んだ" (acting on the Sonozakis' wishes/intent), specifying that relatives acted at the Sonozakis' direction rather than independently, which is absent from the old translation.

TextENGNew: ...No, even the police could deduce this much.
The real work begins from here.
Specifically: who did it, by what means, and for what purpose?
Why did Satoshi-kun have to become a victim?
What was the motive?
Who gave out that order?
Who actually did it?
The mastermind, the perpetrator, and the truth itself --
all of them lie close at hand.
Perhaps they are right behind me, just over my shoulder.
But even on one's own back, not every spot can be easily reached.
There are spots so awkward to reach that you must strain your shoulder joints and clench your teeth just to graze them with your fingertips.
What I seek is hidden in just such a place.
What I am about to record here...
...serves both as notes to organize my thoughts and as a confession of my own regrets.
changeReason: Old translation omits the entire second clause about the notes serving as "a confession of my own regrets" (悔悟を書き記したもの), which is a significant element of the writer's purpose.

TextENGNew: I expect no eyes but my own will ever see these notes.
But if someone other than me should happen to read them...

...it will mean either that I uncovered the truth and discarded these notes as no longer needed,@kor that I fell victim to the "curse of Oyashiro-sama" before reaching my goal, and only these notes survived.
If it is the former, then all is well.
...But if it is the latter...
Then please --
I need your help.
For the sake of this powerless, ordinary girl.
